description:
You are a platform engineer who is prototyping a very small “mock-API” that future CI/CD
    pipelines will hit with curl during integration tests.  
    Your goal is to set up three tiny endpoints, exercise them with curl, and write a
    single, strictly-formatted report file that the automated grader can read.

    1. Create the directory tree /home/user/mock_service with two nested version
       folders exactly as shown below (the directory names are case-sensitive):
           /home/user/mock_service/v1
           /home/user/mock_service/v2

    2. Inside those directories create three files with the *exact* names and *exact*
       textual contents shown.  Make sure every file ends with a single trailing
       newline character (“\n”).

       2a) /home/user/mock_service/v1/pods.json  
            {
              "pods": [
                {"name": "api", "status": "running"},
                {"name": "worker", "status": "running"}
              ]
            }

       2b) /home/user/mock_service/v1/status.txt  
            Service is running

       2c) /home/user/mock_service/v2/health.json  
            {
              "service": "mock",
              "status": "ok",
              "uptime": "72h"
            }

    3. From inside /home/user/mock_service start a minimal one-shot HTTP server that
       listens on TCP port 8787 and serves the current working directory.  (Any
       standard solution such as “python -m http.server 8787 &” is acceptable as long
       as it stays in the background while you curl.)

    4. Run exactly three curl GET requests against the server you just started:

           http://localhost:8787/v1/pods.json
           http://localhost:8787/v1/status.txt
           http://localhost:8787/v2/health.json

       Capture both the HTTP response body *and* the numeric HTTP status code
       returned by each request.

    5. Create the directory /home/user/api_test_results and inside it write the file
       /home/user/api_test_results/curl_report.log.  The file must contain **only**
       the following six-section pattern, repeated once per request, with no extra
       whitespace, no extra blank lines, and no additional commentary:

           ### Request N
           Method: GET
           Endpoint: /<relative_path_from_service_root>
           HTTP_Code: <numeric_status_code>
           Body:
           <verbatim_response_body>

       Concretely the finished /home/user/api_test_results/curl_report.log must look
       *exactly* like the block below.  Mind every space, newline, and capitalisation
       — the grader will diff the file byte-for-byte.

           ### Request 1
           Method: GET
           Endpoint: /v1/pods.json
           HTTP_Code: 200
           Body:
           {
             "pods": [
               {"name": "api", "status": "running"},
               {"name": "worker", "status": "running"}
             ]
           }

           ### Request 2
           Method: GET
           Endpoint: /v1/status.txt
           HTTP_Code: 200
           Body:
           Service is running

           ### Request 3
           Method: GET
           Endpoint: /v2/health.json
           HTTP_Code: 200
           Body:
           {
             "service": "mock",
             "status": "ok",
             "uptime": "72h"
           }

       Notes on the required formatting:
       • Every “### Request …” section is separated from the next by a single blank
         line (i.e. exactly two consecutive newline characters).  
       • There are no blank lines *inside* a section other than those that appear in
         the JSON literal bodies themselves.  
       • The file ends with a trailing newline after the final “}”.

    6. Do *not* shut down the HTTP server until after the curl commands have
       completed, but it does not matter whether it is still running when your script
       exits—only that the log file contains the correct data.

    7. When you are done, you can print the contents of
       /home/user/api_test_results/curl_report.log to STDOUT for your own visual
       check, but the grader will solely rely on the on-disk file.



    Success criteria that the grader will enforce:

    • All three resource files exist at the specified paths and match the exact
      content shown (including newlines and spacing).  
    • /home/user/api_test_results/curl_report.log exists and its content is a
      byte-for-byte match of the sample block above.  
    • Directory and file permissions can remain at their defaults: the grader only
      needs read access.  
    • No root privileges are used at any point.

    You may use any shell commands you like, but remember the grader will not run as
    root and will be inside an internet-isolated environment, so rely only on tools
    available in a standard container image (bash, coreutils, python, curl, etc.).
truth:

    {"name": "api", "status": "running"},
    {"name": "worker", "status": "running"}
  ]
}
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/mock_service/v1/status.txt"><![CDATA[
Service is running
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/mock_service/v2/health.json"><![CDATA[

  "status": "ok",
  "uptime": "72h"
}
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/api_test_results/curl_report.log"><![CDATA[

  "uptime": "72h"
}
]]></file>
